nativeRange.habitat: Warm, nutrient-rich, still or slow-moving freshwater — Amazon-basin oxbow lakes, lagoons, backwater channels, marshes, and the quiet margins of lowland rivers; floats freely on the surface in full sun, often forming dense interlocking mats

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface for the floating rosette and its long trailing roots
open-top tank or pond — emergent leaves rise up to ~30 cm above the waterline and need headroom
strong overhead light to keep growth compact and prevent the rosette from stretching and rotting
low surface agitation — sustained spray or splash wets the bulbous petioles and leaves and causes rot

goodWith: outdoor ponds and tubs in jurisdictions where the plant is legal
livebearer fry and small fish that shelter in the trailing root mass
labyrinth fish (gouramis, bettas) that anchor bubble-nests to the underside
high-bioload tanks needing aggressive nitrate export
avoidWith: any outdoor body of water connected to natural waterways — escape risk is severe and dispersal causes ecosystem collapse
low-light or indoor tanks without strong direct lighting (plant etiolates and rots)
tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or overhead drip
goldfish, large herbivorous cichlids, and other vigorous plant-eaters

imageCandidates.notes: Close-up of the species' signature lavender-purple flower spike — strongest visual identifier for Pontederia crassipes / Eichhornia crassipes. URL pattern follows commons naming; reviewer should confirm exact file before upload.
Product page hosts close-range photos of the bulbous-petiole rosette as sold to hobbyists; useful reference but not directly reusable. Reviewer should source a CC-licensed image instead.

summary: Water Hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) is a free-floating South American plant with glossy rounded leaves on bulbous, air-filled stems and showy lavender-purple flower spikes. It is among the fastest-growing aquatic plants on Earth — and one of the most destructive invasive aquatic weeds worldwide.
careNotes:
Water Hyacinth is straightforward to maintain in the right conditions: strong overhead light, warm water between 20 and 30 °C (the plant can briefly tolerate temperatures down to around 12 °C), no CO2 injection, no substrate, and no supplemental fertilization beyond the nutrients already dissolved in the water. The species thrives on dissolved nitrogen and phosphorus and is exceptionally efficient at pulling both from the water column, which has made it a common addition to koi ponds, outdoor tubs, and high-bioload systems where nutrient export is a priority.

Growth is very fast: mats are known to double in one to two weeks under good conditions, with some populations reported to multiply more than a hundredfold in 23 days. Indoors, the practical challenge runs the other direction — without genuinely strong, direct light, the bulbous petioles (the swollen, air-filled stem segments that keep the plant afloat) elongate and the rosette collapses and rots. Most aquarium-grown specimens decline within weeks unless they receive light comparable to outdoor conditions.

Emergent leaves rise 15 to 30 cm above the waterline and the trailing root mass can extend 30 to 50 cm below the surface, so an open-top tank of at least 110 L (around 30 gallons) or an outdoor pond or tub is the typical setup. Keep surface agitation low: leaves and petioles that stay persistently wet tend to rot at the base.

LEGAL STATUS — READ BEFORE PURCHASE: Water Hyacinth is widely regarded as one of the most destructive invasive aquatic plants in the world. It is a federally listed noxious weed in the United States, regulated by USDA APHIS, and banned across the European Union under invasive species legislation that took effect in 2016. It is prohibited under New Zealand's National Pest Plant Accord and restricted in much of Australia and across parts of Africa and Asia. Within the United States, even where federal commerce is permitted, individual states including Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, and Wisconsin prohibit sale or possession. Verify local law before acquiring this plant. Dispose of excess material by drying it to brittleness and sealing it in a bag for landfill — never release it into any waterway, storm drain, or compost that has runoff potential.
breedingNotes: In cultivation, Water Hyacinth reproduces almost entirely by vegetative stolons — the parent rosette extends horizontal runners that bud off identical daughter plantlets, which detach and become independent plants once the runner decays. This runner-based spread is the mechanism behind the species' well-documented doubling time. Sexual reproduction through the lavender flower spikes is also possible: each spike carries 8 to 15 flowers with six petals and a distinctive central yellow-and-purple marking. The resulting seeds are dispersed by wind and water and remain viable in sediment for 15 to 20 years, which is a major reason eradication efforts in invaded water bodies so frequently fail. For aquarium and pond keepers, the practical step is simply to separate any daughter rosette once it has developed its own root mass.

primary.name: Wikipedia — Pontederia crassipes
primary.notes: Authoritative taxonomy reference: family Pontederiaceae, order Commelinales, modern reclassification from Eichhornia crassipes to Pontederia crassipes (subgenus Oshunae). Provides native range (South American Amazon basin), temperature tolerance (min 12 °C, optimum 25-30 °C, max 33-35 °C), pH 5.0-7.5, salinity ceiling (~15 % seawater), leaf size 10-20 cm, plant height up to ~1 m, flower description (lavender-pink spikes of 8-15 flowers), explosive growth (mat doubling in 1-2 weeks, >100x multiplication in 23 days), and the invasive-status timeline including EU 2016 ban, NZ National Pest Plant Accord, and the 1956-2020 US federal ban history.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Hobby-side cross-check: lighting moderate-to-strong (strong light drives very fast growth), CO2 not necessary, temperature 68-80 °F (20-27 °C), growth very fast, care level very easy, placement floating in ponds/tubs/open-top tanks/sumps, arrival width 7-8 inches (~18-20 cm), recommends 30+ gallon (~110 L) tank or pond. Crucially confirms current US shipping restrictions: 'does not ship to Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, or Wisconsin' — direct evidence for the legally-restricted hobbyist advisory.

propagation: seed from lavender flower spikes (viable 15-20 years in sediment; relevant for invasive spread, not aquarium propagation)

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Eichhornia crassipes is widely regarded as one of the world's worst invasive aquatic plants. It is a US federally listed noxious weed regulated by USDA APHIS, banned across the European Union under invasive species legislation that took effect in 2016, prohibited under New Zealand's National Pest Plant Accord, restricted across much of Australia, and barred from sale or possession in numerous US states including Alabama, Arizona, Florida, Idaho, Mississippi, Nebraska, South Carolina, Texas, and Wisconsin. Check local law before acquiring, and never release plants into any waterway, storm drain, or runoff-exposed compost.
